O kurde, no to chyba to. Czekaj, ale ja ustawiałem dziewiętnaście dwa na nich, przynajmniej tak mi się wydaje... nie, czekaj, robiłem to piątek po południu i mogłem zapomnieć o strefie C.

Rafał Krawczyk 0:40
No to leć sprawdź fizycznie, na wyświetlaczu czujnika zobaczysz jaki baud jest ustawiony. A jak już będziesz to sprawdź też parzystość bo to drugie co może gryzć.

Piotr Grabowski 0:48
Ok, ale nie mogę teraz iść na halę bo jest przerwa produkcyjna do siedemnastej. Zaraz po przerwie lecę.

Rafał Krawczyk 0:54
Dobra. A jak będziesz tam to weź jeszcze multimetrem sprawdź napięcie na szynie, bo jak mamy dużo urządzeń to może być problem z zasilaniem i terminacją.

Piotr Grabowski 1:02
Jasne. A to jeszcze jedno, szef pytał czy da się te dane z Modbusa wrzucić na nowy dashboard, ten co Ania robi w grafanie.

Rafał Krawczyk 1:09
Da się, ale musimy to przepuścić przez ten nasz gateway modbus to MQTT a potem z MQTT do click house no i w grafanie się podpinamy. Ania wie o tym?

Piotr Grabowski 1:17
Nie, pogadam z nią jutro. Ale najpierw naprawmy te czujniki.

Rafał Krawczyk 1:20
Tak, priorytet czujniki. Jak naprawisz baud rate i dalej nie działa to dzwoń do mnie, pogrzebię w konfiguracji gateway'a.

Piotr Grabowski 1:26
Spoko, dzięki.
output:
TEMAT: Awaria komunikacji Modbus z czujnikami ciśnienia na hali produkcyjnej
UCZESTNICY: Piotr Grabowski (automatyk/technik), Rafał Krawczyk (inżynier automatyki)
DATA: 19.03.2026

OBECNY_STAN:
- Timeouty na czujnikach ciśnienia Honeywell w strefach B i C (montowane w zeszłym tygodniu)
- Czujniki widoczne na skanerze Modbus, ale odczyt rejestrów nie działa
- Strefa A (czujniki temperatury) działa poprawnie
- Szyna Modbus skonfigurowana na 19200 baud

USTALENIA:
- Prawdopodobna przyczyna: niezgodność baud rate (Honeywell domyślnie 9600, szyna 19200)
- Możliwe że strefa C nie została przekonfigurowana (konfiguracja robiona w piątek po południu)
- Do sprawdzenia również: parzystość, napięcie na szynie, terminacja

PROBLEMY_I_OBAWY:
- Nie można iść na halę do 17:00 (przerwa produkcyjna)
- Potencjalny problem z zasilaniem szyny przy dużej liczbie urządzeń
- Dashboard w Grafanie wymaga przepuszczenia danych: Modbus → MQTT → ClickHouse → Grafana

DO_ZROBIENIA:
- Piotr: sprawdzić fizycznie baud rate na czujnikach w strefie B i C — po 17:00
- Piotr: sprawdzić parzystość na czujnikach
- Piotr: zmierzyć napięcie na szynie Modbus multimetrem
- Piotr: porozmawiać z Anią o integracji danych Modbus z dashboardem Grafana — jutro
- Rafał: pomoc z konfiguracją gateway'a jeśli fix baud rate nie pomoże

DECYZJE:
- Priorytet: naprawa czujników, dashboard później

ODŁOŻONE:
- Integracja danych Modbus z dashboardem Grafana — po naprawie czujników

input:
Transcript
March 17, 2026, 2:00 PM

Sarah Chen 0:00
Alright everyone, let's get started. We've got the post-mortem for last Thursday's outage. Mike, can you walk us through the timeline?

Mike Rodriguez 0:06
Sure. So at 2:47 AM UTC we got paged, the main API gateway was returning five hundreds across the board. Initial investigation showed that the data base connection pool was exhausted. Turns out the new caching layer that we deployed Wednesday evening had a connection leak.

Sarah Chen 0:20
Ok so the deploy went out Wednesday around what time?

Mike Rodriguez 0:23
Like 6 PM Pacific. And it was fine for about eight hours, then connections started building up gradually. By 2 AM the pool was full.

Sarah Chen 0:32
And the monitoring didn't catch it?

Jess Park 0:34
So here's the thing, we have alerts on connection count but the threshold was set at ninety percent of pool size. The pool was configured for 500 connections but the new caching layer created a second pool that we weren't monitoring. So the monitored pool looked fine while the unmonitored one was leaking.

Sarah Chen 0:48
That's a gap. How did we resolve it?

Mike Rodriguez 0:51
I rolled back the caching layer at 3:15 AM and connections dropped immediately. Service was fully restored by 3:22 AM. Total downtime about 35 minutes.

Sarah Chen 0:59
Ok, so action items. First, Mike, can you fix the connection leak in the caching code? Don't just roll back permanently, we need that feature.

Mike Rodriguez 1:05
Yeah I already found the bug. It's creating a new connection per cache miss instead of reusing from the pool. Should have a fix ready by end of day tomorrow.

Jess Park 1:12
I'll add monitoring for all connection pools, not just the primary. And I think we should add a circuit breaker so if any pool hits eighty percent we start rejecting new connections gracefully instead of just dying.

Sarah Chen 1:22
Love it. Timeline on the monitoring?

Jess Park 1:24
Monitoring by Wednesday, circuit breaker is bigger, maybe end of next week?

Sarah Chen 1:28
That works. One more thing — we need a deploy checklist item that says if you're adding a new connection pool or external dependency, you must add monitoring before it goes to prod.

Mike Rodriguez 1:36
Makes sense. I can add that to our deploy runbook.

Sarah Chen 1:39
Perfect. Anything else? No? Ok let's wrap.
output:
TOPIC: Post-mortem for API gateway outage on March 12, 2026
PARTICIPANTS: Sarah Chen (engineering lead), Mike Rodriguez (backend engineer), Jess Park (SRE/infrastructure)
DATE: 17.03.2026

CURRENT_STATE:
- Outage occurred March 12 at 2:47 AM UTC, lasted ~35 minutes
- Root cause: connection leak in new caching layer deployed Wednesday evening
- Caching layer rolled back, service restored at 3:22 AM UTC
- Bug identified: new connection created per cache miss instead of reusing pool

FINDINGS:
- Caching layer created a second connection pool that wasn't monitored
- Existing alerts only covered primary pool (threshold at 90% of 500)
- Leak built up gradually over ~8 hours before exhausting connections
- No deploy checklist item for verifying monitoring of new dependencies

ISSUES_AND_CONCERNS:
- Monitoring gap: secondary connection pools not tracked
- No circuit breaker for graceful degradation on pool exhaustion
- Deploy process doesn't enforce monitoring for new external dependencies

ACTION_ITEMS:
- Mike: fix connection leak in caching code (reuse pool connections) — by end of day March 18
- Jess: add monitoring for all connection pools — by Wednesday (March 19)
- Jess: implement circuit breaker at 80% pool capacity — end of next week (March 27)
- Mike: add deploy runbook checklist item requiring monitoring for new connection pools/dependencies

DECISIONS:
- Caching feature will be re-deployed after fix (not permanently rolled back)
- Circuit breaker threshold set at 80% pool capacity
- New deploy policy: monitoring required before prod for any new connection pool or external dependency

DEFERRED:
- None

input:
Transcript
March 21, 2026, 10:30 AM

David Kim 0:00
Hey team so I wanted to go over the candidate we interviewed yesterday for the senior front end role. Lisa, you did the technical round right?

Lisa Nguyen 0:07
Yeah so Alex Thompson, five years experience, currently at Shopify. Technical round went well overall. Really strong on react and type script, solved the live coding challenge in like fifteen minutes which is faster than most candidates. Good understanding of performance optimization, talked about virtual scrolling and code splitting without me prompting.

David Kim 0:25
Nice. Any concerns?

Lisa Nguyen 0:27
Two things. First, when I asked about accessibility he was a bit hand wavy, like he knew the basics, ARIA labels and semantic HTML, but didn't seem to have deep experience with screen readers or actual WCAG compliance testing. And we need that for the health care client.

David Kim 0:40
Right, that's non-negotiable given our contracts. What's the second thing?

Lisa Nguyen 0:44
His system design answer was ok but not great. When I asked him to design a real time collaborative editor he went straight to web sockets without considering alternatives or trade offs. I'd expect a senior to at least mention CRDTs or operational transform.

David Kim 0:56
Hmm. Tom, you did the behavioral round?

Tom Walsh 0:59
Yeah and honestly I was impressed. Really good communicator, gave specific examples for everything. The way he talked about mentoring junior devs was genuine, not rehearsed. And he handled the conflict scenario really well, talked about a time he disagreed with his manager about a technical decision and how he presented data to change the outcome.

David Kim 1:14
So we've got strong coding, strong communication, weaker on accessibility and system design. What's the call?

Lisa Nguyen 1:20
I think he's hirable but not at senior level. Maybe mid-level with a clear path to senior? Accessibility is learnable but it's a gap right now.

Tom Walsh 1:28
I disagree actually. His communication and mentoring skills are senior level. You can teach accessibility depth but you can't teach someone to be a good leader. I'd say senior with a ninety day plan that includes accessibility certification.

David Kim 1:40
Interesting. I'm leaning towards Tom's view but Lisa has a point about the healthcare contracts. How about this — we offer senior level but the offer letter includes a condition that he completes IAAP WAS certification within first six months. And we pair him with Maria who's our accessibility expert for the first quarter.

Lisa Nguyen 1:55
That works for me.

Tom Walsh 1:57
Yeah I like that.

David Kim 1:59
Ok I'll draft the offer today and send it to HR. Lisa, can you write up the technical feedback in Greenhouse by end of day?

Lisa Nguyen 2:05
Yep.

David Kim 2:07
And Tom, yours too please.

Tom Walsh 2:09
Will do.

David Kim 2:10
Great, thanks everyone.
output:
TOPIC: Hiring debrief for senior frontend candidate Alex Thompson
PARTICIPANTS: David Kim (hiring manager), Lisa Nguyen (technical interviewer), Tom Walsh (behavioral interviewer)
DATE: 21.03.2026

CURRENT_STATE:
- Candidate: Alex Thompson, 5 years experience, currently at Shopify
- Technical round: strong React/TypeScript, fast live coding, good performance knowledge
- Behavioral round: excellent communication, genuine mentoring examples, good conflict resolution

FINDINGS:
- Strengths: React/TypeScript expertise, performance optimization (virtual scrolling, code splitting), communication, mentoring, leadership qualities
- Weaknesses: shallow accessibility knowledge (basics only, no WCAG compliance testing experience), system design answer lacked depth (websockets only, no CRDTs/OT consideration)
- Accessibility gap is significant given healthcare client contracts requiring WCAG compliance

ISSUES_AND_CONCERNS:
- Accessibility expertise is non-negotiable due to healthcare contracts
- System design answer below senior-level expectations
- Disagreement on leveling: Lisa suggested mid-level, Tom argued senior

ACTION_ITEMS:
- David: draft offer letter with IAAP WAS certification condition — today
- David: send offer to HR
- Lisa: write technical feedback in Greenhouse — by end of day
- Tom: write behavioral feedback in Greenhouse — by end of day
- (Post-hire) Pair Alex with Maria (accessibility expert) for first quarter

DECISIONS:
- Offer at senior level with condition: IAAP WAS certification within 6 months
- Pairing with Maria (accessibility expert) during first quarter
- Senior level justified by leadership/communication skills; accessibility gap addressed via certification requirement

DEFERRED:
- None

input:
Transcript
March 22, 2026, 3:00 PM

James Carter 0:00
Ok so let's talk about the migration. We've got about two hundred thousand users on the old auth system and we need to move them to the new identity provider by end of Q2. Rachel, where are we?

Rachel Adams 0:10
So I've been mapping out the data model differences. The old system stores passwords as MD5 hashes which is obviously terrible, and the new IDP uses bcrypt. So we can't just copy hashes over, we need a lazy migration strategy where users re-hash on next login.

James Carter 0:24
How many users haven't logged in within the last six months?

Rachel Adams 0:27
About forty thousand. Those are the tricky ones because they'll have stale MD5 hashes sitting in the new system until they log in. We either force a password reset for those or we keep the old auth system running in parallel.

James Carter 0:38
I really don't want to keep the old system running, it's a security liability. Kevin, what's the effort on a forced reset flow?

Kevin Brooks 0:44
Not bad actually. We send an email saying hey we've upgraded our security please reset your password, with a tokenized link. I can build that in a week. But we need to coordinate with marketing on the email copy and legal needs to review because of GDPR, we're essentially saying we stored your password insecurely.

Rachel Adams 0:58
We definitely should NOT say that in the email. Just say we're upgrading security. Standard practice.

James Carter 1:04
Agreed. Kevin, work with Rachel on the email copy, keep legal in the loop but the message is positive — security upgrade, not admission of weakness. What about the migration itself, the actual data transfer?

Rachel Adams 1:14
I've got a script that maps ninety percent of the fields automatically. The remaining ten percent are custom attributes that the old system stored as unstructured JSON. I need to go through those manually and figure out what maps where. Estimate two weeks for the mapping plus testing.

James Carter 1:27
Ok. And we need a rollback plan. If the new IDP has issues on day one we need to be able to switch back without users noticing.

Kevin Brooks 1:33
We could run both systems behind a feature flag for the first week. Route ten percent of traffic to new IDP, monitor errors, then ramp up.

Rachel Adams 1:40
Yeah canary makes sense. But that means we need both systems running simultaneously which contradicts what you said about killing the old one James.

James Carter 1:47
For one week it's fine. The goal is to decommission the old system by end of Q2, not necessarily day one. Let's plan for a two week canary period then full cutover. Kevin, can you set up the feature flag infrastructure?

Kevin Brooks 1:57
Yeah, we already use LaunchDarkly so it's straightforward. I can have the flag ready by end of next week.

James Carter 2:03
Alright let's do this. Rachel, start the data mapping. Kevin, build the reset flow and the feature flag. I'll schedule a check-in in two weeks. Any blockers?

Rachel Adams 2:11
I need access to the production database of the old system. Been waiting on a ticket for three days.

James Carter 2:16
I'll escalate that today. Anything else? Ok, good talk.
output:
TOPIC: Planning migration of 200K users from legacy auth to new identity provider
PARTICIPANTS: James Carter (engineering manager), Rachel Adams (backend engineer), Kevin Brooks (backend engineer)
DATE: 22.03.2026

CURRENT_STATE:
- ~200,000 users on legacy auth system, deadline end of Q2 for migration
- Old system uses MD5 password hashes (security liability), new IDP uses bcrypt
- ~40,000 users inactive for 6+ months (stale MD5 hashes, can't lazy-migrate)
- Data mapping script covers 90% of fields; 10% are unstructured JSON custom attributes requiring manual mapping
- Rachel blocked on production database access (ticket pending 3 days)

FINDINGS:
- Lazy migration (re-hash on login) works for active users but not for 40K inactive ones
- Forced password reset chosen over keeping old system running in parallel long-term
- Email messaging: frame as security upgrade, not admission of past weakness
- GDPR review needed for reset email but messaging should be positive
- LaunchDarkly already in use, feature flag infrastructure straightforward

ISSUES_AND_CONCERNS:
- 40K inactive users need forced password reset
- Legal/GDPR review needed for password reset email
- 10% of data fields are unstructured JSON requiring manual mapping (~2 weeks)
- Production database access blocked

ACTION_ITEMS:
- Rachel: start data model mapping and field conversion — 2 weeks for mapping + testing
- Kevin: build forced password reset flow (tokenized email link) — 1 week
- Kevin: set up LaunchDarkly feature flag for canary routing — by end of next week
- Kevin + Rachel: collaborate on reset email copy, keep legal in the loop
- James: escalate Rachel's production database access ticket — today
- James: schedule 2-week check-in

DECISIONS:
- Forced password reset for inactive users (no long-term parallel systems)
- 2-week canary period: start with 10% traffic to new IDP, ramp up, then full cutover
- Old auth system decommissioned by end of Q2 (not day one)
- Reset email framed as security upgrade, not security admission

DEFERRED:
- None

input:
Transkript
20. März 2026, 14:00

Stefan Müller 0:00
Gut, fangen wir an. Wir haben das Quarterly Review für das Warehouse Management System. Claudia, wie sieht's aus mit den KPIs?

Claudia Weber 0:06
Also, die Durchlaufzeit hat sich um fünfzehn Prozent verbessert gegenüber letztem Quartal. Das liegt hauptsächlich an der neuen Pick-Reihenfolge Optimierung die wir im Januar deployed haben. Aber die Fehlerquote bei der Kommissionierung ist gestiegen, von ein Komma zwei auf ein Komma acht Prozent.

Stefan Müller 0:20
Das ist nicht gut. Woher kommt das?

Claudia Weber 0:22
Ich hab mir die Daten angeschaut und es korreliert mit dem neuen Barcode Scanner. Die alten Zebra Scanner hatten eine Leserate von neunundneunzig Komma fünf Prozent, die neuen DataLogic schaffen nur achtundneunzig Komma eins. Und wenn ein Barcode nicht gelesen wird, greifen die Mitarbeiter manuell ein und da passieren Fehler.

Stefan Müller 0:38
Warte, wer hat die DataLogic bestellt? Ich dachte wir bleiben bei Zebra.

Markus Bauer 0:42
Das war Einkauf, die haben gewechselt weil DataLogic dreißig Prozent günstiger war. Ich hab damals gesagt dass wir erstmal testen sollten aber das wurde überstimmt.

Stefan Müller 0:50
Ok, da müssen wir mit dem Einkauf reden. Claudia, kannst du einen Report zusammenstellen der zeigt wie viel uns die höhere Fehlerquote kostet? Retourkosten, Nacharbeitszeit und so weiter. Dann haben wir Argumente.

Claudia Weber 0:59
Ja mach ich, bis Freitag.

Stefan Müller 1:02
Markus, und du schau mal ob wir bei DataLogic ein Firmware Update bekommen das die Leserate verbessert. Vielleicht ist es ja nur ein Softwareproblem.

Markus Bauer 1:10
Klar, ich ruf morgen deren Support an.

Stefan Müller 1:13
Gut. Noch was anderes — das Thema Inventur. Wir müssen dieses Jahr auf zyklische Inventur umstellen, Stichtags geht nicht mehr wegen der neuen Lagergröße. Claudia, hast du dir das System angeschaut?

Claudia Weber 1:24
Ja, unser WMS unterstützt zyklische Inventur aber das Modul ist nicht aktiviert. Ich brauche ne Lizenz Erweiterung, kostet ungefähr zwölftausend Euro pro Jahr, und dann muss ich die Zählzonen konfigurieren. Aufwand schätz ich drei Wochen mit Tests.

Stefan Müller 1:37
Die Lizenz genehmige ich. Wann kannst du anfangen?

Claudia Weber 1:40
Nach dem Scanner-Thema, also Mitte April realistisch.

Stefan Müller 1:44
Ok, passt. Noch Fragen? Gut, dann Danke.
output:
THEMA: Quartals-Review Warehouse Management System — KPIs und Inventurumstellung
TEILNEHMER: Stefan Müller (Abteilungsleiter), Claudia Weber (WMS-Administratorin/Analystin), Markus Bauer (IT/Hardware)
DATUM: 20.03.2026

AKTUELLER_STAND:
- Durchlaufzeit um 15% verbessert (dank Pick-Reihenfolge-Optimierung vom Januar)
- Kommissionier-Fehlerquote gestiegen: 1,2% → 1,8%
- Neue DataLogic-Scanner (30% günstiger als Zebra) haben niedrigere Leserate: 98,1% vs. 99,5%
- WMS unterstützt zyklische Inventur, Modul nicht aktiviert (Lizenzerweiterung ~12.000€/Jahr nötig)

FESTSTELLUNGEN:
- Fehlerquote korreliert mit Wechsel von Zebra auf DataLogic Scanner
- Bei fehlgeschlagenem Scan greifen Mitarbeiter manuell ein → Fehlerquelle
- Scanner-Wechsel wurde vom Einkauf entschieden, Test wurde vorgeschlagen aber überstimmt
- Zyklische Inventur notwendig wegen neuer Lagergröße (Stichtaginventur nicht mehr machbar)

PROBLEME_UND_BEDENKEN:
- Höhere Fehlerquote verursacht Kosten (Retouren, Nacharbeit)
- Einkauf hat Scanner ohne ausreichende Tests gewechselt
- Möglicherweise Firmware-Problem bei DataLogic

AUFGABEN:
- Claudia: Report über Kosten der höheren Fehlerquote (Retouren, Nacharbeit) — bis Freitag
- Markus: DataLogic Support kontaktieren wegen Firmware-Update — morgen
- Stefan: Gespräch mit Einkauf über Scanner-Entscheidung (mit Claudias Report)
- Stefan: Lizenzerweiterung für zyklische Inventur genehmigen
- Claudia: Zyklische Inventur konfigurieren (Lizenz + Zählzonen + Tests, ~3 Wochen) — Start Mitte April

ENTSCHEIDUNGEN:
- Lizenz für zyklische Inventur genehmigt (12.000€/Jahr)
- Umstellung auf zyklische Inventur statt Stichtaginventur

ZURÜCKGESTELLT:
- Endgültige Entscheidung Scanner (Zebra vs. DataLogic) — abhängig von Kostenreport und Firmware-Test

input:
Transcription
18 mars 2026, 15:00

Sophie Martin 0:00
Bon, on commence. On a le point sur le projet de refonte du site e-commerce. Pierre, où en est-on côté back end?

Pierre Dubois 0:06
Alors, l'API de catalogue est quasiment finie, il me reste les filtres avancés et la recherche full text. J'ai intégré elastic search mais j'ai un souci de performance sur les requêtes avec facettes, ça prend plus de deux secondes sur le catalogue complet.

Sophie Martin 0:18
Deux secondes c'est beaucoup trop. Le cahier des charges dit moins de cinq cents millisecondes. C'est quoi le problème exactement?

Pierre Dubois 0:25
En fait le mapping elastic est pas optimal, j'ai mis tous les champs en nested et ça explose les temps de requête quand on a beaucoup de documents. Faut que je refasse le mapping avec du flattened pour les attributs produits.

Sophie Martin 0:35
Ok, et ça prend combien de temps?

Pierre Dubois 0:37
Le mapping lui même c'est une journée, mais après faut réindexer tout le catalogue et ça c'est euh trois quatre heures de processing. Puis tester les requêtes. Allez, deux jours max.

Sophie Martin 0:45
Ok. Amélie, côté front?

Amélie Leroy 0:48
Le design system est en place, les composants React sont faits pour le catalogue et la fiche produit. Par contre j'ai un problème avec le panier, le state management est un cauchemar parce qu'on a trois sources de vérité — le local storage, l'API et le state Redux. Faut qu'on décide d'une source de vérité unique.

Sophie Martin 1:03
Oui c'est critique. T'as une recommandation?

Amélie Leroy 1:06
Je dirais l'API comme source de vérité avec un cache optimiste côté client. On écrit toujours vers l'API et on met à jour le state local en optimistic update. Si l'API répond avec une erreur on rollback.

Pierre Dubois 1:16
Ça me va, je peux ajouter un endpoint temps réel avec server sent events pour pousser les mises à jour du panier.

Sophie Martin 1:23
Parfait. Amélie combien de temps pour refactorer le panier?

Amélie Leroy 1:26
Une semaine, tests compris.

Sophie Martin 1:29
Ok. Dernière chose, le client veut une démo le trente et un mars. On sera prêts?

Pierre Dubois 1:34
Si je finis elastic d'ici vendredi oui.

Amélie Leroy 1:37
Le panier sera pas fini pour la démo mais le catalogue et la fiche produit seront nickel.

Sophie Martin 1:42
Bon, on fait la démo sans le panier si nécessaire, on montrera catalogue et produit. Le panier on le montre à la démo suivante. Tout le monde est ok? Bien, merci.
output:
SUJET: Point d'avancement — refonte site e-commerce (API, front-end, démo client)
PARTICIPANTS: Sophie Martin (cheffe de projet), Pierre Dubois (développeur back-end), Amélie Leroy (développeuse front-end)
DATE: 18.03.2026

ÉTAT_ACTUEL:
- API catalogue quasi finalisée, reste filtres avancés et recherche full-text
- ElasticSearch intégré mais performance insuffisante (>2s vs. objectif <500ms) à cause du mapping nested
- Design system React en place, composants catalogue et fiche produit terminés
- Panier: problème d'architecture — 3 sources de vérité (localStorage, API, Redux)

CONSTATS:
- Mapping ElasticSearch avec champs nested cause des temps de requête excessifs
- Solution: passer aux champs flattened pour les attributs produits (~2 jours)
- Architecture panier: API comme source unique de vérité avec cache optimiste côté client
- SSE (Server-Sent Events) pour mises à jour temps réel du panier

PROBLÈMES_ET_PRÉOCCUPATIONS:
- Performance ElasticSearch non conforme au cahier des charges
- Refactoring panier prendra 1 semaine — ne sera pas prêt pour la démo du 31 mars

À_FAIRE:
- Pierre: refaire mapping ElasticSearch (flattened) + réindexation + tests — 2 jours (objectif: vendredi)
- Pierre: ajouter endpoint SSE pour mises à jour panier en temps réel
- Amélie: refactorer le panier (source unique API + optimistic updates) — 1 semaine
- Sophie: organiser démo client le 31 mars (catalogue + fiche produit uniquement)

DÉCISIONS:
- API = source de vérité unique pour le panier, avec optimistic updates côté client
- Démo du 31 mars sans le panier — catalogue et fiche produit seulement
- Panier présenté à la démo suivante

REPORTÉ:
- Présentation du panier au client — prochaine démo (après le 31 mars)

input:
Transcripción
19 de marzo de 2026, 11:00

Carlos Herrera 0:00
Bueno, empezamos. Tenemos que definir la arquitectura del nuevo sistema de reservas. Ana, tú ya revisaste los requisitos verdad?

Ana García 0:06
Sí, a ver, el cliente quiere un sistema que maneje reservas de salas de reuniones para sus tres oficinas, Madrid, Barcelona y Valencia. Necesita calendario en tiempo real, notificaciones por correo, y integración con su directorio activo para autenticación.

Carlos Herrera 0:18
Ok. Y cuántos usuarios concurrentes estamos hablando?

Ana García 0:21
Dicen que unos quinientos empleados pero en pico no más de cien simultáneos. No es una carga bestia pero necesitan que sea rápido, menos de un segundo para hacer una reserva.

Carlos Herrera 0:30
Luis, alguna preferencia de stack?

Luis Fernández 0:33
Yo propondría punto net con blazor server para el front porque ya tenemos experiencia y el equipo del cliente también usa punto net. Para la base de datos postgreSQL, y para tiempo real signal ar que se integra nativo.

Carlos Herrera 0:45
Me gusta. Pero blazor server tiene el tema de las conexiones persistentes con web sockets, con cien usuarios simultáneos no debería ser problema pero hay que prever.

Luis Fernández 0:54
Sí, podemos usar Azure Signal R Service como backplane y eso escala sin problema. Además si en el futuro quieren más oficinas ya estamos preparados.

Ana García 1:00
Una cosa, el cliente mencionó que tiene un sistema legacy de reservas en un excel compartido en sharepoint. Quieren que migremos los datos históricos.

Carlos Herrera 1:08
Ay dios, un excel. Ok cuántos registros?

Ana García 1:11
No sé exactamente pero llevan como tres años usándolo así que bastantes. Le pido el archivo para analizar.

Carlos Herrera 1:17
Sí porfa. Luis, en paralelo tú arranca con el esqueleto del proyecto, el template de blazor con autenticación y la configuración de la base de datos. Así cuando tengamos el excel ya podemos diseñar la migración.

Luis Fernández 1:27
Perfecto, eso me lleva unos tres días tener el boilerplate listo con CI CD incluido.

Ana García 1:32
Y yo mientras preparo el documento de arquitectura con los diagramas para que el cliente lo apruebe antes de que empecemos en serio.

Carlos Herrera 1:38
Bien. Reunión de seguimiento el viernes. Para entonces quiero el boilerplate, el documento de arquitectura, y el excel del cliente. Algo más? No? Perfecto.
output:
TEMA: Definición de arquitectura para nuevo sistema de reservas de salas
PARTICIPANTES: Carlos Herrera (tech lead), Ana García (analista/PM), Luis Fernández (desarrollador)
FECHA: 19.03.2026

ESTADO_ACTUAL:
- Requisitos recogidos: reservas de salas para 3 oficinas (Madrid, Barcelona, Valencia)
- ~500 empleados, ~100 concurrentes en pico
- Sistema legacy: Excel compartido en SharePoint (~3 años de datos históricos)
- Necesario: calendario en tiempo real, notificaciones email, integración Active Directory

ACUERDOS:
- Stack: .NET + Blazor Server (front) + PostgreSQL (BD) + SignalR (tiempo real)
- Azure SignalR Service como backplane para escalabilidad
- Tiempo de respuesta objetivo: <1 segundo para hacer una reserva
- Migración de datos históricos desde Excel de SharePoint necesaria

PROBLEMAS_Y_PREOCUPACIONES:
- Blazor Server usa conexiones WebSocket persistentes (manejable con 100 usuarios pero hay que prever)
- Datos históricos en Excel — cantidad desconocida, formato posiblemente inconsistente

TAREAS:
- Luis: crear boilerplate del proyecto (Blazor + auth + BD + CI/CD) — 3 días
- Ana: solicitar archivo Excel de reservas históricas al cliente
- Ana: preparar documento de arquitectura con diagramas para aprobación del cliente
- Carlos: reunión de seguimiento el viernes (boilerplate + documento + Excel esperados)

DECISIONES:
- .NET/Blazor Server elegido por experiencia del equipo y del cliente
- Azure SignalR Service para escalabilidad futura (más oficinas)
- Documento de arquitectura debe ser aprobado por el cliente antes de comenzar desarrollo

APLAZADO:
- Diseño de migración de datos — pendiente del análisis del Excel

TEMAT: Fałszywe alarmy czujników dymu w budynku B — analiza i plan naprawy

- Po kalibracji przywrócenie progu do 30%
- Dodanie alertu wczesnego ostrzegania: >25% opacity przez >1h = powiadomienie

PROBLEMY_I_OBAWY:
- Drift czujników z powodu braku kalibracji
- Podniesienie progu = ryzyko przegapienia prawdziwego pożaru
- Restarty sterownika prawdopodobnie spowodowane nadmiarem eventów z fałszywych alarmów
- Firmware wymaga przetestowania na sterowni testowym przed wgraniem

DO_ZROBIENIA:

- Ewa: dodać alert wczesnego ostrzegania (>25% przez >1h)
- Ewa: przywrócić próg do 30% po kalibracji
- Damian: pobrać firmware 3.5 i przetestować na sterowni testowym — przed weekendem
- Damian: kalibracja czujników + aktualizacja firmware — weekend
- Piotr: poinformować facility managera o planie naprawy

DECYZJE:
- Tymczasowy próg 35% (kompromis bezpieczeństwo vs fałszywe alarmy)
- Kalibracja + update firmware w weekend
- Alert prewencyjny na 25% opacity

input:
Transcript
March 17, 2026, 2:00 PM

Sarah Chen 0:00
Alright everyone, let's get into the post-mortem for last night's outage. James, can you walk us through what happened?

James O'Brien 0:06
Sure. So around 11 PM eastern, we started getting pages from cloud watch about elevated error rates on the payment service. Five hundred errors spiked from like point one percent to about forty percent in maybe two minutes.

Sarah Chen 0:18
What triggered it?

James O'Brien 0:20
So we traced it back to a config change that got deployed at like ten fifty. Someone updated the connection pool settings in the payment service, dropped max connections from fifty to five.

Sarah Chen 0:30
Who deployed that?

James O'Brien 0:32
It was in a batch of config changes, I don't think anyone realized the payment service config was in there. The PR had like twenty files and the reviewer probably didn't catch it.

Lisa Park 0:40
Yeah I was the reviewer and honestly twenty config files in one PR, I missed it. My bad.

Sarah Chen 0:45
Ok no blame, let's focus on how we prevent this. How long was the outage?

James O'Brien 0:49
About forty five minutes. I got paged, saw the connection pool thing, reverted the config and it recovered in about five minutes after that.

Lisa Park 0:57
The problem is our rollback took too long. We had to go through the full deployment pipeline to revert a config change. That's insane.

Sarah Chen 1:03
Agreed. So action items. First, config changes should be separate from code changes, separate PRs.

James O'Brien 1:09
Yeah and we need a fast path for config rollbacks. Like a one click revert that doesn't go through the full CICD pipeline.

Lisa Park 1:15
I'd also add that we need better alerts. We got the five hundred error alert but we should have gotten an alert when the connection pool config changed to something below a threshold.

Sarah Chen 1:23
Good point. Config validation rules. James, can you look into adding pre deploy checks for critical configs?

James O'Brien 1:29
Yeah, I can set up open policy agent or something similar. Give me a week.

Sarah Chen 1:33
Lisa, can you set up the config change alerting?

Lisa Park 1:36
Sure, I'll have that by wednesday.

Sarah Chen 1:38
And I'll write up the new policy about separating config and code PRs. Let's also add a max PR size or at least a warning when a PR has more than five files.

James O'Brien 1:45
One more thing — we should probably have a runbook for payment service outages. I knew what to do because I've been here three years but if a new person got paged they'd be lost.

Sarah Chen 1:52
Great call. James, add that to your list. Ok I think we're good. I'll send out the post-mortem doc by end of day.

Lisa Park 1:58
Sounds good.

James O'Brien 2:00
Yep.
output:
TOPIC: Post-mortem for payment service outage
PARTICIPANTS: Sarah Chen (engineering lead), James O'Brien (senior SRE), Lisa Park (DevOps engineer)
DATE: 17.03.2026

CURRENT_STATE:
- Payment service outage occurred March 16, ~11 PM ET, lasted ~45 minutes
- 500 errors spiked from 0.1% to 40%
- Root cause: connection pool max connections changed from 50 to 5 in a config batch deploy at 10:50 PM
- Config change was buried in a 20-file PR and missed during review
- Manual revert took ~40 min (had to go through full CI/CD pipeline), service recovered in 5 min after revert

FINDINGS:
- Config changes mixed with code changes in single PRs are risky
- No fast-path rollback mechanism for config changes
- No config validation or alerting on critical parameter changes
- No runbook for payment service outages

ISSUES_AND_CONCERNS:
- Full CI/CD pipeline for config rollback is too slow for incidents
- Large PRs (20 files) make thorough review impractical
- New team members would not know how to handle payment service outage

ACTION_ITEMS:
- James: set up pre-deploy config validation (OPA or similar) — 1 week
- James: create payment service outage runbook
- Lisa: set up config change alerting (connection pool threshold alerts) — by Wednesday
- Sarah: write policy for separating config and code PRs
- Sarah: add warning/limit for PRs with more than 5 files
- Sarah: send post-mortem doc — end of day

DECISIONS:
- Config changes must go in separate PRs from code changes
- Need a fast-path config rollback (bypass full CI/CD)
- Pre-deploy validation rules for critical configs

DEFERRED:
- None

input:
Transcript
March 21, 2026, 3:30 PM

David Miller 0:00
Ok let's talk about the API gateway migration. We've been on Kong for two years and there's been a push to move to envoy. Rachel, you've been researching this, what's your take?

Rachel Kim 0:08
So I've been running envoy in our staging environment for about three weeks now. Performance is better, we're seeing about twenty percent lower latency on p99. But the configuration is significantly more complex. Kong we could configure through the admin API, envoy needs yaml manifests and it's not exactly user friendly.

David Miller 0:22
Twenty percent on p99 is significant though. Kevin, from an ops perspective?

Kevin Walsh 0:26
I have concerns. Our team is small and everyone knows Kong. Envoy learning curve is steep. And we'd need to rewrite all our custom plugins. We have what, seven custom plugins?

Rachel Kim 0:35
Eight actually. And yeah rewriting them for envoy means lua or wasm filters. That's probably a month of work just for the plugins.

David Miller 0:42
A month? That's a lot. Can we do it incrementally?

Rachel Kim 0:45
We could run both in parallel. Route non critical traffic through envoy first, keep critical stuff on Kong until we're confident. But running two gateways adds operational complexity.

Kevin Walsh 0:53
I'd rather do it all at once honestly. The parallel approach means maintaining two systems, two sets of monitoring, two sets of runbooks. With three people on the ops team that's rough.

David Miller 1:00
Ok so what if we push this to Q3? That gives us time to train the team on envoy, rewrite plugins gradually, and do a proper cutover.

Rachel Kim 1:08
That works for me. I'd suggest we allocate like twenty percent time starting next sprint for envoy prep work. Plugin rewrites, documenting config patterns, setting up monitoring.

Kevin Walsh 1:16
I can live with that. But I want a rollback plan. If we cut over and something goes wrong we need to be able to go back to Kong within minutes not hours.

David Miller 1:23
Absolutely. Rachel, can you own the migration plan? Include the rollback strategy, plugin rewrite timeline, training schedule.

Rachel Kim 1:29
Sure. I'll have a draft by end of next week.

David Miller 1:32
Kevin, start looking into envoy monitoring. What metrics we need, how it integrates with our grafana setup.

Kevin Walsh 1:37
Will do. Oh one more thing — we should check if our service mesh play changes anything. If we go with Istio later it comes with envoy built in so we'd be ahead.

David Miller 1:45
Good point. Let's keep that in mind but don't let it drive this decision. Ok I think we have a plan. Thanks everyone.
output:
TOPIC: API gateway migration evaluation — Kong to Envoy
PARTICIPANTS: David Miller (engineering manager), Rachel Kim (platform engineer), Kevin Walsh (ops lead)
DATE: 21.03.2026

CURRENT_STATE:
- Running Kong API gateway for 2 years with 8 custom plugins
- Envoy tested in staging for 3 weeks — 20% lower p99 latency
- Envoy config significantly more complex (YAML manifests vs Kong admin API)
- Ops team is 3 people, all experienced with Kong, not Envoy

FINDINGS:
- Envoy performance advantage is significant (~20% p99 improvement)
- Plugin rewrite estimated at ~1 month (Lua or WASM filters)
- Parallel gateway approach adds too much operational burden for small team
- Potential Istio adoption later would include Envoy — migration work wouldn't be wasted

ISSUES_AND_CONCERNS:
- Steep Envoy learning curve for ops team
- 8 custom plugins need full rewrite
- Running two gateways in parallel too complex for 3-person ops team
- Need reliable rollback plan (minutes, not hours)

ACTION_ITEMS:
- Rachel: draft full migration plan (rollback strategy, plugin rewrite timeline, training schedule) — by end of next week
- Rachel: allocate ~20% time from next sprint for Envoy prep work
- Kevin: research Envoy monitoring setup (metrics, Grafana integration)
- Team: begin gradual plugin rewrites and Envoy training

DECISIONS:
- Migration pushed to Q3 (no rush, do it properly)
- Big-bang cutover preferred over parallel running
- 20% time allocation for Envoy prep starting next sprint
- Rollback plan is mandatory requirement

DEFERRED:
- Istio/service mesh decision — noted as potential synergy but not driving this migration
- Specific plugin rewrite order and priority

input:
Transcript
March 22, 2026, 11:00 AM

Mark Thompson 0:00
Let's go through the candidate review for the senior backend position. We interviewed three people last week. Emily, you were on all three panels, right?

Emily Foster 0:07
Yes. So candidate one was Alex Rivera. Strong system design, really good at talking through trade offs. The distributed systems question he nailed. But his coding exercise was weak. Took too long and the code was messy.

Mark Thompson 0:18
How weak are we talking?

Emily Foster 0:20
Like he got to a working solution but it took forty five minutes for what should've been a twenty minute problem. And no tests. Said he usually writes tests after which, fine, but in an interview you'd think...

Mark Thompson 0:30
Ok. Candidate two?

Emily Foster 0:32
Preethi Sharma. Amazing coder. She did the exercise in fifteen minutes, clean code, edge cases handled, tests included. But in the system design round she struggled. Kept proposing over engineered solutions. Like for a simple CRUD app she was talking about event sourcing and CQRS.

Mark Thompson 0:45
She's only got three years experience right? That might be a seniority thing.

Emily Foster 0:48
Yeah fair. She could grow into the design stuff. And honestly her code quality was the best I've seen in months.

Mark Thompson 0:54
And candidate three?

Emily Foster 0:56
Jordan Chen. Solid across the board. Not the best at anything but no weaknesses either. Four out of five in coding, system design, communication. Very steady.

Mark Thompson 1:05
So where do we land?

Emily Foster 1:07
I'd go with Preethi honestly. The coding skill is harder to teach than system design. And we have enough senior architects to mentor her on the design side.

Mark Thompson 1:14
I'm torn between Preethi and Jordan. Jordan's more well rounded and we need someone who can hit the ground running.

Emily Foster 1:20
True but Jordan's ceiling might be lower. Preethi has that raw talent that's really rare.

Mark Thompson 1:25
Ok here's what I'm thinking. Let's extend an offer to Preethi for the senior role. And let's reach out to Jordan about the mid level position that's opening next month. He'd be great there.

Emily Foster 1:33
That's a good call actually. Alex?

Mark Thompson 1:36
Pass on Alex. Good conversationalist but the coding gap is too big for a senior role.

Emily Foster 1:41
Agreed. I'll send the feedback to HR today.

Mark Thompson 1:44
Great. Thanks Emily.
output:
TOPIC: Candidate review for senior backend position
PARTICIPANTS: Mark Thompson (hiring manager), Emily Foster (interviewer/panel lead)
DATE: 22.03.2026

CURRENT_STATE:
- Three candidates interviewed last week for senior backend role
- All three went through coding exercise + system design round
- Separate mid-level position opening next month

FINDINGS:
- Alex Rivera: strong system design and communication, weak coding (45 min for 20-min problem, messy code, no tests)
- Preethi Sharma: exceptional coder (15 min, clean, tests included), weak system design (over-engineered solutions), 3 years experience
- Jordan Chen: solid across the board (4/5 in all areas), no weaknesses but no standout strengths

ISSUES_AND_CONCERNS:
- Preethi's system design weakness may be a seniority/experience gap (3 years)
- Jordan is well-rounded but may have lower growth ceiling
- Alex's coding gap too large for senior role despite strong design skills

ACTION_ITEMS:
- Emily: send interview feedback to HR — today
- HR (implied): extend offer to Preethi Sharma for senior backend role
- HR (implied): reach out to Jordan Chen about mid-level position opening next month

DECISIONS:
- Offer senior backend role to Preethi Sharma — coding talent is rare, design skills can be mentored
- Offer Jordan Chen mid-level position (opening next month)
- Pass on Alex Rivera — coding gap too significant for senior role

DEFERRED:
- None

input:
Transkript
19. März 2026, 14:00

Stefan Müller 0:00
Gut, fangen wir an. Wir müssen über das neue Lagerverwaltungssystem sprechen. Frau Schneider, wie ist der aktuelle Stand?

Anja Schneider 0:06
Also wir haben das Grundsystem aufgesetzt. Die Datenbank läuft, die API ist eee grundsätzlich fertig. Aber wir haben ein Problem mit der Barcode Scanner Integration. Die Scanner von Zebra, die wir im Lager haben, die kommunizieren über ein proprietäres Protokoll und die Dokumentation ist auf Chinesisch.

Stefan Müller 0:20
Auf Chinesisch? Ernsthaft?

Anja Schneider 0:22
Ja, also es gibt eine englische Version aber die ist veraltet. Version zwei punkt eins und wir haben Scanner mit Firmware vier punkt null. Die API hat sich komplett geändert.

Stefan Müller 0:31
Haben wir den Zebra Support kontaktiert?

Anja Schneider 0:33
Ja, Ticket ist offen seit zwei Wochen. Keine Antwort ausser einer automatischen Bestätigung.

Martin Weber 0:38
Ich hätte da eine Idee. Wir könnten einen Wrapper schreiben der das Zebra Protokoll in eee standard HTTP Requests übersetzt. Ich habe so was ähnliches mal für einen anderen Kunden gemacht.

Stefan Müller 0:47
Wie lange würde das dauern?

Martin Weber 0:49
Drei vier Tage wenn die Grundstruktur des Protokolls klar ist. Anja, kannst du mir die chinesische Doku schicken? Ich nutze einfach deepl oder so.

Anja Schneider 0:56
Klar, schick ich dir nach dem Meeting.

Stefan Müller 0:58
Ok, und was ist mit der Bestandserfassung? Der Kunde will ja dass alle zwanzigtausend Artikel bis Ende des Monats im System sind.

Anja Schneider 1:05
Das schaffen wir nicht manuell. Wir brauchen einen CSV Import. Ich kann das bauen, aber ich brauche das Datenformat vom Kunden.

Stefan Müller 1:12
Ich frag heute noch beim Kunden nach. Martin, kannst du parallel zum Wrapper auch die Validierungslogik für den Import schreiben?

Martin Weber 1:18
Ja, aber dann wird der Wrapper eher eine Woche. Beides gleichzeitig ist knapp.

Stefan Müller 1:23
Ok, Priorität ist der Wrapper. CSV Import können wir notfalls auch nächste Woche machen. Noch was?

Anja Schneider 1:28
Ja, der Kunde hat gefragt ob wir auch eine mobile App machen können für die Lagerarbeiter. Zum Scannen und Bestand prüfen.

Stefan Müller 1:34
Das war nicht im Angebot. Ich klär das kaufmännisch. Erstmal Fokus auf das Kernsystem.

Martin Weber 1:39
Verstanden.

Stefan Müller 1:40
Gut, dann sind wir durch.
output:
THEMA: Statusbesprechung neues Lagerverwaltungssystem
TEILNEHMER: Stefan Müller (Projektleiter), Anja Schneider (Backend-Entwicklerin), Martin Weber (Entwickler)
DATUM: 19.03.2026

AKTUELLER_STAND:
- Grundsystem aufgesetzt, Datenbank und API grundsätzlich fertig
- Barcode-Scanner-Integration blockiert: Zebra-Scanner nutzen proprietäres Protokoll, Dokumentation nur auf Chinesisch (englische Version veraltet: v2.1, Scanner haben Firmware v4.0)
- Zebra-Support-Ticket seit 2 Wochen offen, keine Antwort
- 20.000 Artikel müssen bis Monatsende im System erfasst werden

FESTLEGUNGEN:
- Wrapper für Zebra-Protokoll → Standard-HTTP als Lösung für Scanner-Integration
- CSV-Import für Massenerfassung der Artikel nötig
- Priorität: Wrapper vor CSV-Import

PROBLEME_UND_BEDENKEN:
- Zebra-Dokumentation veraltet/chinesisch, Support reagiert nicht
- Manuelle Erfassung von 20k Artikeln bis Monatsende unrealistisch
- Datenformat vom Kunden für CSV-Import noch ausstehend
- Martin kann Wrapper und Validierung nicht parallel schaffen

AUFGABEN:
- Martin: Wrapper Zebra-Protokoll → HTTP schreiben — ~1 Woche (Priorität)
- Anja: chinesische Doku an Martin schicken — heute
- Anja: CSV-Import bauen (nach Erhalt des Datenformats) — nächste Woche
- Stefan: Datenformat beim Kunden anfragen — heute
- Stefan: mobile App kaufmännisch klären (nicht im Angebot)

ENTSCHEIDUNGEN:
- Wrapper hat Priorität vor CSV-Import
- CSV-Import wird ggf. auf nächste Woche verschoben

ZURÜCKGESTELLT:
- Mobile App für Lagerarbeiter — nicht im Angebot, muss kaufmännisch geklärt werden

input:
Transcription
20 mars 2026, 10:00

Julien Moreau 0:00
Bon, on fait le point sur le projet de migration cloud. Sophie, t'en es où?

Sophie Laurent 0:04
Alors j'ai fini la partie terrafor euh terraform pour l'infrastructure AWS. Les VPC, les subnets, les security groups, tout est codé. Par contre j'ai un souci avec le RDS. La base de données actuelle fait deux téraoctets et le temps de migration estimé c'est genre quarante huit heures.

Julien Moreau 0:18
Quarante huit heures? On peut pas se permettre deux jours de downtime.

Sophie Laurent 0:22
Non c'est pour ça que je regarde la réplication. On pourrait mettre en place un DMS euh Database Migration Service pour faire une réplication continue et basculer quand c'est synchronisé.

Thomas Petit 0:30
Ouais mais attention avec DMS j'ai eu des problèmes l'année dernière sur un autre projet. Les triggers et les stored procedures passent pas automatiquement. Faut les migrer à la main.

Sophie Laurent 0:38
On en a combien des stored procedures?

Thomas Petit 0:40
Euh bonne question. Je dirais une centaine? Dont certaines qui font du crosstabase euh cross-database. Celles là vont poser problème.

Julien Moreau 0:48
Ok. Thomas, tu peux faire un inventaire des stored procedures? Avec un classement genre critique, important, peut attendre?

Thomas Petit 0:54
Oui je fais ça cette semaine.

Julien Moreau 0:56
Sophie, continue sur le DMS. Fais un test avec une copie de la base pour valider que la réplication fonctionne.

Sophie Laurent 1:02
D'accord. Par contre il me faut un accès à l'environnement de staging AWS. J'ai fait la demande à l'équipe infra mais ils ont pas encore répondu.

Julien Moreau 1:10
Je relance aujourd'hui. Autre chose — le client veut savoir si on tient toujours le planning de fin avril.

Thomas Petit 1:16
Fin avril c'est chaud. La migration des stored procs à elle seule ça peut prendre deux trois semaines.

Sophie Laurent 1:22
Je suis d'accord. Je dirais mi-mai si tout se passe bien.

Julien Moreau 1:26
Ok je préviens le client qu'on risque un décalage de deux semaines. Mieux vaut prévenir maintenant que surprendre après. On se revoit jeudi pour faire le point sur l'inventaire de Thomas.

Thomas Petit 1:34
Ça marche.

Sophie Laurent 1:35
Ok.
output:
SUJET: Point d'avancement migration cloud AWS
PARTICIPANTS: Julien Moreau (chef de projet), Sophie Laurent (ingénieure cloud/infra), Thomas Petit (DBA/backend)
DATE: 20.03.2026

ÉTAT_ACTUEL:
- Infrastructure AWS codée en Terraform (VPC, subnets, security groups)
- Base de données actuelle: 2 To, migration directe estimée à 48h (downtime inacceptable)
- ~100 stored procedures dont certaines cross-database
- Accès staging AWS en attente (demande faite à l'équipe infra, sans réponse)
- Planning initial: fin avril — risque de décalage à mi-mai

CONSTATS:
- DMS (Database Migration Service) envisagé pour réplication continue et bascule sans downtime
- DMS ne migre pas automatiquement les triggers et stored procedures
- Les stored procedures cross-database vont poser des problèmes spécifiques
- Migration des stored procs estimée à 2-3 semaines

PROBLÈMES_ET_PRÉOCCUPATIONS:
- 48h de downtime inacceptable pour migration directe
- DMS ne gère pas triggers/stored procedures automatiquement
- Stored procedures cross-database nécessitent un traitement manuel
- Planning fin avril probablement irréaliste — mi-mai plus réaliste
- Accès staging bloqué

À_FAIRE:
- Thomas: inventaire des stored procedures avec classification (critique/important/peut attendre) — cette semaine
- Sophie: test DMS avec copie de la base sur environnement staging
- Julien: relancer l'équipe infra pour accès staging — aujourd'hui
- Julien: prévenir le client du risque de décalage de 2 semaines
- Réunion de suivi jeudi (inventaire de Thomas)

DÉCISIONS:
- Utiliser DMS pour réplication continue plutôt que migration directe
- Prévenir le client proactivement du décalage potentiel

REPORTÉ:
- Rien explicitement reporté

input:
Transcripción
18 de marzo de 2026, 16:00

Carlos Ruiz 0:00
Bueno, empezamos. Tenemos que hablar del tema del rendimiento de la app. María, qué encontraste?

María García 0:05
A ver, hice un profiling completo del backend y el problema principal es la consulta de reportes. La query que genera el ORM para el reporte mensual hace como seis joins y tarda veinte segundos en ejecutarse. Con cien mil registros es inaceptable.

Carlos Ruiz 0:18
Veinte segundos? Dios mío. Y el frontend?

María García 0:21
El frontend también tiene lo suyo. El bundle de java script pesa tres megas y no hay lazy loading. Todo se carga de golpe.

Carlos Ruiz 0:28
Ok. Pablo, tú revisaste la infraestructura?

Pablo Fernández 0:31
Sí. El servidor de base de datos está al ochenta por ciento de CPU constantemente. Creo que necesitamos eee o escalar verticalmente o meter un read replica para las consultas de reportes.

Carlos Ruiz 0:40
Cuánto costaría un read replica?

Pablo Fernández 0:42
Unos trescientos dólares al mes más o menos. Pero antes de gastar plata yo intentaría optimizar la query. Muchas veces el problema es que el ORM genera consultas horribles y con una query nativa puedes bajar de veinte segundos a uno.

María García 0:52
Sí, ya lo intenté. Con una query nativa y un índice compuesto bajé a tres segundos. Pero el arquitecto dice que no quiere queries nativas en el código, que todo tiene que ser por el ORM.

Carlos Ruiz 1:00
Bueno, tres segundos es mejor que veinte pero sigue siendo lento para un reporte. Y si precalculamos los datos?

María García 1:06
Un materialized view? Eso podría funcionar. Lo actualizamos cada hora y el reporte consulta la vista en vez de las tablas base. Eso bajaría a milisegundos.

Pablo Fernández 1:14
Me gusta esa idea. Y no rompe la regla del ORM porque el ORM simplemente consulta una vista.

Carlos Ruiz 1:19
Perfecto. María, puedes implementar el materialized view? Y Pablo, lo del bundle de tres megas, necesitamos que alguien lo vea.

Pablo Fernández 1:26
Eso es más de frontend. Yo puedo meter un análisis con lajthaus euh lighthouse y webpack bundle analyzer para identificar qué pesa tanto. Pero el refactor lo tendría que hacer Diego que es el frontender.

Carlos Ruiz 1:35
Ok. María, materialized view esta semana. Pablo, análisis del bundle y pasas resultados a Diego. Y del tema del read replica, lo dejamos por ahora?

Pablo Fernández 1:43
Sí, primero veamos si con la vista materializada baja la carga del servidor. Si baja del ochenta por ciento ya no necesitamos réplica.

Carlos Ruiz 1:49
Bien. Nos vemos el viernes para revisar avances.

María García 1:52
Dale.

Pablo Fernández 1:53
Ok.
output:
TEMA: Problemas de rendimiento de la aplicación — backend, frontend e infraestructura
PARTICIPANTES: Carlos Ruiz (PM/lead), María García (backend dev), Pablo Fernández (infra/DevOps)
FECHA: 18.03.2026

ESTADO_ACTUAL:
- Query de reporte mensual tarda 20 segundos (6 joins generados por ORM, 100k registros)
- Con query nativa + índice compuesto baja a 3 segundos (pero política prohíbe queries nativas)
- Bundle JavaScript pesa 3 MB sin lazy loading
- Servidor de BD al 80% CPU constantemente

HALLAZGOS:
- ORM genera consultas ineficientes para reportes complejos
- Materialized view actualizada cada hora resolvería el problema sin romper la regla del ORM
- Read replica costaría ~$300/mes pero puede no ser necesaria si se optimiza la query
- Bundle necesita análisis con Lighthouse y webpack bundle analyzer

PROBLEMAS_Y_PREOCUPACIONES:
- 20 segundos por reporte es inaceptable
- Política del arquitecto prohíbe queries nativas en el código
- Bundle de 3 MB sin lazy loading afecta carga inicial
- CPU del servidor al 80% — riesgo de saturación

TAREAS:
- María: implementar materialized view para reportes — esta semana
- Pablo: análisis del bundle (Lighthouse + webpack bundle analyzer) y pasar resultados a Diego
- Diego (implícito): refactor del bundle frontend según análisis
- Reunión de seguimiento el viernes

DECISIONES:
- Usar materialized view (actualización horaria) en vez de query nativa
- No implementar read replica por ahora — evaluar después de la vista materializada
- Respetar la política de no queries nativas

APLAZADO:
- Read replica — evaluar si sigue siendo necesaria después de implementar materialized view

W poprzedniej firmie robiliśmy dual write, znaczy pisaliśmy do starego i nowego schematu jednocześnie i potem przełączaliśmy. Ale to dużo roboty.

No, za dużo jak na ten sprint. Zrobię batche, powinno wystarczyć. Okno serwisowe mamy w niedzielę 2-8 rano.

A testujesz wcześniej na stagingu?

Tak, dzisiaj puszczę na stagingu z pełnymi danymi. Ok, coś jeszcze?

A ten bug z logowaniem co klient zgłosił?

A tak, zapomniałem. Klient mówi że po 15 minutach go wyrzuca, a token powinien żyć godzinę. Rafał, możesz zerknąć? Pewnie refresh token nie działa.

No to, mamy tu ten problem z czujnikami na hali B. Jarek, opowiedz co się dzieje.

Signy jest, eee, to znaczy Siemens S7-1200 na hali B raportuje temperatury ale co jakiś czas mamy skoki, jakby z 22 stopni nagle na 85 i z powrotem. I to wyzwala alarm w SCADA no i operatorzy już mają dość fałszywych alarmów bo leci SMS o 3 w nocy.

Dashboard w Grafanie mam w połowie. Dane idą do Influx DB, eee InfluxDB, wizualizacja podstawowa jest. Brakuje eksportu do CSV i alertów mailowych. Tydzień roboty.

DECYZJE:
